column1: # Principle: A list-bound def splices its body inline at the use site.
# When a name is bound via `def NAME [body]` (where body is an
# Eval=true list — the parser/tokenizer flag for normal source-code
# lists), every subsequent occurrence of `NAME` does NOT push a list
# value. Instead the engine REMOVES the word from the token stream
# and INSERTS the body's elements in its place — the body's tokens
# then execute against the live stack.
# This is the foundational definition mechanism for reusable code
# fragments in concatenative languages: a name is shorthand for "run
# this sequence of tokens here". Forth's `:` definitions, Joy's
# words, and Factor's combinator definitions all share this design.
# AQL adopts it via def + list-body.
# Rules being asserted
# 1. `def NAME [body]` registers a binding under NAME pointing at the
#    list. The list is unquoted (Eval=true, NOT Quoted=true).
# 2. A subsequent `NAME` reference is REPLACED with the body's
#    elements — the splice happens in stepWord's def-stack lookup
#    branch (engine.go:754-760):
#        if top.VType.Equal(TList) && !top.IsTypedList() &&
#           !top.IsTableType() && !top.Quoted {
#            elems := top.AsList()
#            expanded := make([]Value, elems.Len())
#            copy(expanded, elems.Slice())
#            e.stackSplice(e.pointer, 1, expanded...)
#            return nil
# 3. The body executes against the CURRENT stack — there is no
#    sandbox. So a body like `[dup add]` requires a value on the
#    stack at the use site; `def doub [dup add] doub 5` errors
#    because the splice fires before `5` is pushed.
# 4. The splice is at the USE SITE, not at definition time. The body
#    is captured by reference: redefining a word later does NOT
#    affect already-spliced occurrences (because the splice already
#    happened — they're literal tokens now).
# 5. Composition works naturally: a body can reference other defs.
#    On splice, those names spawn their own splice/dispatch paths.
# 6. Quoted lists (`def x quote [body]`) bypass the splice entirely
#    — they're stored as data values. `x` then resolves to the list
#    value, not its body.
# Why this matters
# This is the difference between a "data list" and a "code list".
# Without the splice rule, `def doub [dup add]` would just push
# `[dup add]` as a list value when `doub` is referenced — useless
# as a function definition. The Eval=true / Quoted=true flag pair
# is what discriminates the two intents.
# CLAUDE.md "Quotation System" describes the rule in prose; this
# file pins the executable form.
# Companion files: fn.tsv covers typed-param fn-bodies (different
# splice path via installSpecFnDef / dispatchFnDef); autoeval.tsv
# covers the Eval=true vs Quoted=true distinction.
# tokens
# Basic splice: body executes against stack containing the input.
def doub [ dup add ] 5 doub
column3: 5 pushed; doub splices [dup add]; dup→[5,5]; add→[10]

column1: def square [ dup mul ] 7 square
def inc [ 1 add ] 5 inc
column3: body adds 1 to top of stack — embedded literal in body

column1: # Body with embedded literals — curried form.
def add5 [ add 5 ] 10 add5
column3: body uses an embedded constant; 10 add 5 = 15

column1: def double_then_inc [ dup add 1 add ] 5 double_then_inc
# The use-site contract: body executes against the live stack, not
# a sandbox. Insufficient stack at use site → error.
def doub [ dup add ] doub 5
column3: splice fires before 5 is pushed; dup needs 1 stack value, sees nothing

column1: def needs2 [ add ] needs2 1 2
column3: body is just [add]; splice → add 1 2 → 3 (add forward-collects)

column1: def needs2 [ add ] 1 2 needs2
column3: body splice still works with prefix args

column1: def needs2 [ add ] needs2
column3: splice fires; add waits for forward args; nothing follows; insufficient args

column1: # Composition: one def calling another.
def doub [ dup add ] def quad [ doub doub ] 3 quad
def inc [ 1 add ] def add2 [ inc inc ] 5 add2
# Splice happens at use, not definition. Note however that x in the
# example below is unreachable: doub's body splices BEFORE x is
# resolved, so the body's `dup` runs against an empty stack and errors.
# This is a real footgun: list-body defs are eager about splicing.
def x 5 def doub [ dup add ] doub x
column3: doub splices [dup add] at point; dup needs 1 stack value, sees empty stack — x is never reached

column1: # Parens isolate splice scopes — each splice runs in its sub-engine.
def doub [ dup add ] ( 3 doub ) ( 5 doub )
column3: without the parens the inner add would greedily grab the second invocation's argument

column1: # Quoted-list def is data, not code.
def lst quote [ 1 2 3 ] length lst
column3: lst resolves to the quoted list; length sees a 3-element list, returns 3

column1: def lst quote [ 1 2 3 ] first lst
# Empty-body def is a no-op when invoked.
def noop [ ] 5 noop
column3: splice removes the word and inserts nothing; 5 stays

column1: def noop [ ] noop
column3: bare invocation produces an empty stack (renders as the empty string)
